g.data-narration: Cloud Data Encryption is the last line of defense in cloud computing, protecting data even if infrastructure is compromised.
g.text: Transforming Data Security in the Cloud

g.data-narration: Encryption transforms readable plaintext into unreadable ciphertext using algorithms and keys, ensuring data remains confidential.
The shared responsibility model defines that providers secure infrastructure while customers encrypt their data within the cloud.
Key cryptography terms include plaintext (readable data), ciphertext (encrypted data), encryption algorithms, and encryption keys.
Symmetric encryption uses one secret key for both encryption and decryption. It's fast but faces key distribution challenges.
g.text: Same key for encryption and decryption - like a single key for a lock
Very fast, efficient for large data

g.data-narration: Asymmetric encryption uses key pairs: public key encrypts, private key decrypts. Solves key distribution but is slower.
g.text: Key pair: public key encrypts, private key decrypts - like a mailbox

g.data-narration: Encryption at Rest protects stored data on disks, databases, and object storage using FDE, file-level, or database encryption.
g.text: Protects data stored on persistent media - hard drives, SSDs, databases, object storage

g.data-narration: Encryption in Transit protects data moving between endpoints using TLS/SSL protocols, combining asymmetric and symmetric encryption.
g.text: Protects data moving between endpoints using TLS/SSL
TLS combines asymmetric (session establishment) + symmetric (bulk data encryption)

g.data-narration: Encryption in Use protects data during processing in memory, the most challenging state, using Confidential Computing and TEEs.
g.text: Most challenging state - protecting data while being processed in memory
Data decrypted in RAM for computation

g.data-narration: Key management is critical - KMS provides managed key services, CMK offers customer control, and Cloud-HSM provides FIPS-validated security.
g.text: The security of encrypted data depends entirely on key security
AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, GCP Cloud KMS

g.data-narration: AWS provides encryption for S3 (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C), EBS volumes (AES-256), and centralized key management via AWS KMS.
Azure offers Storage Service Encryption for Blob Storage, Transparent Data Encryption for SQL Database, and Azure Key Vault for key management.
g.text: Used by cloud apps and services

g.data-narration: GCP provides default encryption for Cloud Storage, Customer-Supplied Keys for Compute Engine, and Cloud KMS for centralized key management.
Best practices include encrypting by default, classifying data, controlling keys, enforcing TLS, monitoring usage, rotating keys, and planning for key loss.
Continue with monitoring, key rotation, and key loss planning as essential encryption best practices.
